data science II midterm project

Muxin Li

2026-03-24

library(tidyverse)

library(mgcv)

library(patchwork)

library(earth)

library(pdp)
## 
## Attaching package: 'pdp'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     partial
load("datA.RData")

datA= datA|>
  mutate(
    menopause = factor(menopause),
    race = factor(race),
    tumor_grade = factor(tumor_grade),
    ER = factor(ER),
    PR = factor(PR),
    HER2 = factor(HER2),
    stage = factor(stage),
    surgery = factor(surgery),
    chemo = factor(chemo),
    hormonal_therapy = factor(hormonal_therapy)
  )

(a)

GAM model

step 1: choose linear terms and nonlinear terms briefly.

menopause, race, tumor_grade, ER, PR, HER2, stage, surgery, chemo, hormonal_therapy are categorical variables. Usually, we can treat as linear terms. age, bmi, tumor_size, lymph_nodes, Ki67 we need to find whether they are linear or nonlinear

p1 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= age, y= log_PFS)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2) + geom_smooth(method="loess")
p2 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= bmi, y= log_PFS)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2) + geom_smooth(method="loess")
p3 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= tumor_size, y= log_PFS)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2) + geom_smooth(method="loess")
p4 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= lymph_nodes, y= log_PFS)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2) + geom_smooth(method="loess")
p5 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= Ki67, y= log_PFS)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2) + geom_smooth(method="loess")

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) / p5
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

comment: from the plot we find age is linear, lymph_nodes is linear, bmi has a clear curve (first rising and then falling), tumor_size and Ki67 are slightly nonlinear

step 2: choose smooth term exactly.

we are not sure tumor_size and Ki67 are smoothing terms, so we use ANOVA to compare

m1 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

m2 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data =datA
)

m_tumor = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + Ki67 +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage +
    surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

m_ki67 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage +
    surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

anova(m1, m2, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                              
## 2    1974.7     952.34 1.4481   1.6994 2.4353 0.1047
anova(m1, m_tumor, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                              
## 2    1974.7     952.34 1.4481   1.6994 2.4353 0.1047
anova(m1, m_ki67, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev         Df    Deviance F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                                
## 2    1976.1     954.04 7.4776e-09 -1.3358e-10

comment: Although exploratory plots suggested mild nonlinearity for tumor_size and Ki67, model comparisons using ANOVA indicate that smoothing terms do not significantly improve model fit. In particular, the smoothing term for Ki67 has effectively zero degrees of freedom, suggesting a linear relationship. Thus, both variables are retained as linear terms.

step 3: summary the model and and simplify

gam_fit1= gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage +
    surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)
summary(gam_fit1)
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## 
## Parametric coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.7210154  0.1110669  24.499  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.0041846  0.0019153  -2.185 0.029024 *  
## tumor_size        -0.0617453  0.0198687  -3.108 0.001913 ** 
## lymph_nodes       -0.0659591  0.0097351  -6.775 1.63e-11 ***
## Ki67              -0.0038725  0.0019844  -1.951 0.051143 .  
## menopause1         0.0396511  0.0468592   0.846 0.397557    
## race2              0.0372369  0.0454275   0.820 0.412487    
## race3              0.0641768  0.0561219   1.144 0.252960    
## race4             -0.0388745  0.0481849  -0.807 0.419892    
## tumor_grade2      -0.0453329  0.0463990  -0.977 0.328677    
## tumor_grade3      -0.1633398  0.0593064  -2.754 0.005938 ** 
## ER1                0.2344966  0.0515997   4.545 5.84e-06 ***
## PR1               -0.0048347  0.0387555  -0.125 0.900735    
## HER21             -0.1717877  0.0386554  -4.444 9.32e-06 ***
## stage2             0.0543338  0.0519110   1.047 0.295378    
## stage3             0.0931217  0.1030664   0.904 0.366364    
## surgery1          -0.0090098  0.0332095  -0.271 0.786187    
## chemo1            -0.0002041  0.0324330  -0.006 0.994980    
## hormonal_therapy1  0.1338895  0.0397960   3.364 0.000782 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Approximate significance of smooth terms:
##          edf Ref.df     F p-value    
## s(bmi) 3.905  4.883 7.211 2.8e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## R-sq.(adj) =  0.213   Deviance explained = 22.2%
## GCV = 0.48814  Scale est. = 0.48255   n = 2000

comemnt: Ki67, menopause, race, tumor_grade2, PR, stage, surgery, chemo are not significant, so we can delect . R^2= 0.214

gam_fit2 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

summary(gam_fit2)
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + 
##     ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy
## 
## Parametric coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.616320   0.090610  28.875  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.002942   0.001343  -2.190 0.028622 *  
## tumor_size        -0.049378   0.012886  -3.832 0.000131 ***
## lymph_nodes       -0.063182   0.008538  -7.400 1.99e-13 ***
## tumor_grade2      -0.074679   0.042897  -1.741 0.081857 .  
## tumor_grade3      -0.228148   0.045541  -5.010 5.93e-07 ***
## ER1                0.260201   0.042756   6.086 1.39e-09 ***
## HER21             -0.174936   0.038492  -4.545 5.83e-06 ***
## hormonal_therapy1  0.136634   0.039529   3.457 0.000559 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Approximate significance of smooth terms:
##          edf Ref.df     F p-value    
## s(bmi) 3.885  4.859 7.067   4e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## R-sq.(adj) =  0.214   Deviance explained = 21.9%
## GCV = 0.48529  Scale est. = 0.48217   n = 2000
anova(gam_fit1,gam_fit2, test= "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + 
##     ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev      Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                               
## 2    1986.1     958.12 -10.024  -4.0845 0.8445 0.5858

comment: Although the reduced model has slightly higher residual deviance, the difference is not statistically significant (p = 0.5858). This suggests that the excluded variables do not contribute meaningfully to model performance. Therefore, the simpler model is preferred, achieving a better balance between model fit and complexity. We choose gam_fit2 as final gam model.

plot(gam_fit2)

LM model

step 1: build the LM model

lm_fit= lm(
  log_PFS ~  age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

summary(lm_fit)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + 
##     tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy, data = datA)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.82120 -0.48336  0.01155  0.46708  2.73052 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.579825   0.124840  20.665  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.002880   0.001354  -2.127 0.033532 *  
## bmi                0.001406   0.003462   0.406 0.684597    
## tumor_size        -0.051536   0.012989  -3.968 7.52e-05 ***
## lymph_nodes       -0.061792   0.008604  -7.182 9.69e-13 ***
## tumor_grade2      -0.078086   0.043245  -1.806 0.071118 .  
## tumor_grade3      -0.228888   0.045920  -4.985 6.75e-07 ***
## ER1                0.260743   0.043093   6.051 1.72e-09 ***
## HER21             -0.172948   0.038802  -4.457 8.77e-06 ***
## hormonal_therapy1  0.138938   0.039830   3.488 0.000497 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7003 on 1990 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2041, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2005 
## F-statistic:  56.7 on 9 and 1990 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

step 2: compare with GAM model

set.seed(123)

folds = createFolds(datA$log_PFS, k = 10, list = TRUE)

cv_mse_lm  = numeric(10)
cv_mse_gam = numeric(10)

for(i in 1:10) {
  valid_idx = folds[[i]]
  
  cv_train = datA[-valid_idx, ]
  cv_valid = datA[valid_idx, ]
  
  lm_cv = lm(
    log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes +
      tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
    data = cv_train
  )
  
  gam_cv = gam(
    log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes +
      tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
    data = cv_train
  )
  
  pred_lm  = predict(lm_cv, newdata = cv_valid)
  pred_gam = predict(gam_cv, newdata = cv_valid)
  
  cv_mse_lm[i]  = mean((cv_valid$log_PFS - pred_lm)^2)
  cv_mse_gam[i] = mean((cv_valid$log_PFS - pred_gam)^2)
}

mse_lm= mean(cv_mse_lm)
mse_lm
## [1] 0.4930619
mse_gam= mean(cv_mse_gam)
mse_gam
## [1] 0.4857952

comment: Under 10-fold cross-validation, the GAM model achieved a slightly lower mean squared error (0.486) compared to the linear model (0.493), indicating improved predictive performance. This suggests that modeling bmi with a nonlinear smooth term provides a modest benefit.

Penalized Regression Model

step 1: find the x and y

x = model.matrix(
  log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 +
    stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)[, -1]

y = datA$log_PFS

step 2: build Lasso and Ridge

set.seed(123)

cv_lasso= cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)

plot(cv_lasso)

cv_lasso$lambda.min
## [1] 0.01401631
cv_ridge = cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)

plot(cv_ridge)

step 3: compare with other model

pred_lasso = predict(cv_lasso, x, s = "lambda.min")
pred_ridge = predict(cv_ridge, x, s = "lambda.min")

mse_lasso = mean((y - pred_lasso)^2)
mse_ridge = mean((y - pred_ridge)^2)

mse_lasso
## [1] 0.4882188
mse_ridge
## [1] 0.487001

comment: Among the penalized regression models, Ridge achieved a slightly lower MSE (0.487) than Lasso (0.488), indicating marginally better predictive performance. For mse: GAM < Ridge < Lasso < LM

MARS Model

step 1: find the best model

ctrl1 = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10)

mars_grid = expand.grid(
  degree = 1:3,
  nprune = 2:15
)

set.seed(123)

mars_fit = train(
  log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA,
  method = "earth",
  tuneGrid = mars_grid,
  trControl = ctrl1
)

ggplot(mars_fit)
## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
## ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the caret package.
##   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/topepo/caret/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

mars_fit$bestTune
##    nprune degree
## 10     11      1
coef(mars_fit$finalModel)
##       (Intercept)  h(6-lymph_nodes)               ER1       h(bmi-23.9) 
##        2.09077991        0.07748798        0.22755947       -0.02110819 
##       h(23.9-bmi)      h(Ki67-33.8)             HER21 hormonal_therapy1 
##       -0.05003047        0.02610809       -0.18465081        0.13632405 
##      tumor_grade3 h(tumor_size-0.4)      h(Ki67-24.1) 
##       -0.11666085       -0.04882760       -0.01789249

comment: The optimal MARS model has degree = 1, indicating that no interaction terms are needed and the model is additive. And the best model has 11 basis functions

step 2 : get mse to compare with other model

best_rmse_mars = mars_fit$results |>
  dplyr::filter(
    nprune == mars_fit$bestTune$nprune,
    degree == mars_fit$bestTune$degree
  ) |>
  dplyr::pull(RMSE)

best_mse_mars = best_rmse_mars^2
best_mse_mars
## [1] 0.4948591

comment: to compare with other model we choose MSE, and find mse for MARS is 0.4948591. GAM < Ridge < Lasso < LM < MARS

Different model MSE

model_mse = data.frame(
  Model = c("LM", "GAM", "Ridge", "Lasso", "MARS"),
  MSE = c(mse_lm, mse_gam, mse_ridge, mse_lasso, best_mse_mars))|>
  arrange(MSE)

model_mse
##   Model       MSE
## 1   GAM 0.4857952
## 2 Ridge 0.4870010
## 3 Lasso 0.4882188
## 4    LM 0.4930619
## 5  MARS 0.4948591

comment: In this analysis, the generalized additive model (GAM) achieved the lowest mean squared error, indicating that modeling smooth nonlinear relationships—particularly for BMI—provides the greatest improvement in prediction accuracy. Ridge and Lasso regression offered modest improvements over the linear model by stabilizing coefficients and performing variable selection, but their gains were limited. In contrast, the MARS model, which captures nonlinearity through piecewise linear functions, performed worse than the GAM, suggesting that the underlying relationships are smooth rather than threshold-based.
